solo_maintainer.verbatim_sentence: Note: pbakaus accounts for 97% of all commits. This is a solo-maintainer project — all governance findings should be read in that context.

entries.title: Solo maintainer (96.9% commit share, CODEOWNERS = `* @pbakaus`) with zero branch protection on a 24k-star, 5.5-month-old project
entries.action_hint: Pin to a specific commit SHA (not `@latest`) when installing skills; review the generated .claude/skills/ tree against the upstream source before running them in a sensitive repo.
entries.threat_model_context: pbakaus holds 96.9% of contributor share. CODEOWNERS = `* @pbakaus` and CODEOWNERS for both the build system (`scripts/`) and skill source (`source/`). All 7 governance signals are negative: classic branch protection HTTP 404, 0 rulesets, 0 rules-on-default-branch, owner is a User account (not an Org → no org rulesets), repo not indexed by OSSF Scorecard. 19 of 49 closed PRs (38.8%) self-merged. The blast radius if the maintainer's single key is compromised: every user who runs `npx impeccable skills install` (or the GitHub-Actions-published Cloudflare bundle) after the malicious push receives the malicious skill content directly into 13 separate agent harnesses. 24.3k stars + 1.3k forks indicates a non-trivial install base. Tessl Skill Review + Copilot code review CI workflows provide automated PR review but no human approval gate is enforced — pbakaus can self-merge any PR (and routinely does).
entries.what_this_means: There is no structural second-human gate between pbakaus pushing to `main` and 13 different AI harnesses across the install base receiving updated skill content. The repo's governance posture is 'trust pbakaus, trust Tessl/Copilot review on a best-effort basis.'
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of malicious behavior. Paul Bakaus is a known frontend-design figure (formerly Google, head of design tooling) with a public reputation that itself is a trust signal. The finding is structural — about the absence of a second-human review gate — not about the maintainer.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: enable a branch protection ruleset on `main` requiring ≥1 approving review (Tessl + Copilot are CI-side, not gating); promote the existing CODEOWNERS to a required-review rule; consider transferring ownership to a GitHub Org so `org_rulesets` becomes available. Consumer-side: pin the install command to a specific commit SHA (e.g. `npx skills add pbakaus/impeccable#444e4ac`) rather than `@latest`; or `git clone` + inspect skill `.md` files before linking them into a sensitive repo.

entries.title: `new Function()` build-time RCE in 3 build scripts (issue #92 OPEN 22 days; fix-PR authored, not merged)
entries.duration_label: OPEN 22 days as of scan date
entries.action_hint: Affects contributors who run `bun run build` or `bun run dev` against a malicious commit. Does NOT affect consumers running `npx impeccable detect` or `npm install impeccable`. Wait for #92 to merge before contributing code changes; safe to install + use the published CLI.
entries.threat_model_context: Three build scripts (`scripts/build.js:122`, `scripts/build-extension.js:63`, `scripts/lib/sub-pages-data.js:107`) extract the `ANTIPATTERNS` array from `src/detect-antipatterns.mjs` via regex match, then evaluate the matched string with `new Function()`. Any commit that mutates the matched region grants arbitrary code execution at: (a) `bun run build` (build.js + build-extension.js) — affects every contributor running a local build or release script; (b) `bun run dev` (sub-pages-data.js runs at server boot) — affects every contributor running the dev server. The fix is straightforward (the array is already exported via ESM and can be imported directly), and PR #92 implements it. The PR has been open 22 days. The companion meta-issue #94 catalogs this as 'High' along with two Medium items (selector escaping #93 — already fixed, and execSync with shell interpolation in `bin/commands/skills.mjs` — Low risk, no PR yet).
entries.what_this_means: If a malicious or honest-mistake commit lands in `main` modifying the regex-matched ANTIPATTERNS region, every contributor who runs `bun run build` or `bun run dev` afterward executes attacker-controlled JavaScript in their dev environment with their full file-system access. The published npm package and the released skills bundle are NOT affected by this issue (the eval only runs at build time on the source repo).
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a vulnerability in the published `impeccable` npm package — installed users running `npx impeccable detect` or `npx impeccable live` do not invoke the build scripts. Not a runtime issue. The reported impact is bounded to repository contributors and CI build jobs.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: merge PR #92, which replaces the 3 `new Function()` calls with direct ESM imports of the already-exported `ANTIPATTERNS` array. Add a CI lint that fails on any new `new Function(` or `eval(` call in `scripts/`. Consumer-side: no action required; the published CLI is unaffected.

entries.title: Three-hop install supply chain (impeccable npm → vercel-labs/skills npm → GitHub or impeccable.style CDN) with no signature verification
entries.action_hint: If you only need the CLI scanner, run `npx impeccable detect <files>` directly — no skills install required, no third-party intermediary. If you do want skills, prefer the `git clone` + manual link path over `npx impeccable skills install`.
entries.threat_model_context: Install path 1 (recommended in README): `npx impeccable skills install` runs the published `impeccable` CLI, which then `execSync('npx skills add pbakaus/impeccable')` — this hands trust to vercel-labs/skills (a third-party npm CLI) which fetches the skill content from GitHub. Compromise of vercel-labs/skills allows injection of malicious skill content for every consumer who triggers an install. Install path 2: `npx impeccable skills update` fetches `https://impeccable.style/api/download/bundle/universal` over HTTPS with no signature verification (no SHA, no GPG, no Sigstore — just TLS to the impeccable.style Cloudflare Pages origin), then unpacks with `execSync('unzip -qo "${tmpZip}" -d "${tmpDir}"')`. Compromise of Cloudflare Pages or DNS hijack of impeccable.style allows skill substitution. The `impeccable.style` domain is owned by Paul Bakaus and hosted on Cloudflare Pages per CLAUDE.md L70 — the trust scope of the CDN path is still pbakaus, but the threat surface includes Cloudflare account compromise. Install path 3 (Claude Code plugin marketplace via `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`) follows whatever trust model Anthropic's plugin marketplace uses (single-source from this GitHub repo).
entries.what_this_means: Two of the three skill-install paths route through trust intermediaries beyond pbakaus: vercel-labs/skills (an upstream npm CLI bug per CLAUDE.md L529) and the impeccable.style CDN bundle. Either intermediary's compromise affects every consumer who picks that path. The CLI-only `npx impeccable detect` path does NOT pull skill content and is single-hop (npm registry → consumer).
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that vercel-labs/skills or impeccable.style is compromised; both are reputable. The finding is about install-path trust topology — multi-hop paths have more compromise surfaces than single-hop. Not a vulnerability in `impeccable` itself; a property of the documented install instructions.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: ship signed bundles (Sigstore or detached PGP signatures) and document signature verification as a step in `npx impeccable skills install`; consider removing the npx-skills-add path in favor of a single first-party install verb. Consumer-side: prefer `git clone https://github.com/pbakaus/impeccable && cd impeccable && bun run build` followed by manually copying the relevant `.<harness>/skills/` dir into your repo — this stays within pbakaus's trust scope without involving vercel-labs/skills or the CDN bundle path.

entries.title: No published GHSA, no SECURITY.md, no dependabot.yml — security-fixes ship silently from the consumer's perspective
entries.action_hint: Don't rely on Dependabot to alert you when a security fix ships. Watch the repo's commits or `security:`-prefixed issue closes if you've installed the skill bundle or the CLI.
entries.threat_model_context: 0 GHSA published despite 4 visible security-tagged issues being actively triaged: 2 closed within ~7-10 days in late April 2026 (#100 shell-injection in `npx impeccable [target]` + port-squatting in `live`; #93 selector-escaping in devtools), and 2 open as of scan date (#92 build-time RCE 22d open with fix-PR authored; #94 audit tracking issue 22d open). No SECURITY.md means no public channel for coordinated disclosure (the maintainer uses GitHub Issues with `security:` prefix as an ad-hoc channel). No `.github/dependabot.yml` means dependency patches arrive via manual PR rather than automated bot. The consequence for downstream consumers: no Dependabot alert when a fix ships, no GHSA in npm advisories database, no email from the GitHub Security Advisories pipeline. You only learn about security fixes by reading the repo's commit log or release notes manually.
entries.what_this_means: Security issues ARE handled (and handled fast — 7-10 days for #100/#93), but no signal flows to the broader ecosystem. If you've installed `impeccable` via npm or via a skill-install, you will not receive automated notification when a security fix lands; you'd need to subscribe to repo commits or check the maintainer's changelog at impeccable.style manually.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that vulnerabilities are being hidden — the security-tagged issues are public, the fix PRs are public, the closing commits are public. The gap is in the notification channel (GHSA + Dependabot), not the transparency of the fixes themselves.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add a `SECURITY.md` with a disclosure-channel email or HackerOne handle; add `.github/dependabot.yml` for npm + GitHub Actions; for fix #100 and #93 (already closed), file retroactive GHSAs via `gh api -X POST repos/pbakaus/impeccable/security-advisories` so the npm advisory database picks them up. Consumer-side: subscribe to the repo's commit feed or releases page to catch security fixes; pin the npm package to a specific version and check release notes before bumping.

entries.title: 468 skill files × 13 harness directories form the post-install instruction surface — single source of trust, but large amplification factor
entries.action_hint: Audit `source/skills/impeccable/` (the single source of truth) before installing — not the per-harness fanout, which is regenerated mechanically. The total instruction surface is 36 skill files × ~lines-each, not 468 × ~lines.
entries.threat_model_context: Each `.md` file under any of the 13 `<harness>/skills/<command>/` subtrees becomes part of an LLM's instruction context when its harness loads the skill. 36 unique skills × 13 harness fanouts = 468 skill files in the working tree. CLAUDE.md L96 documents that the harness output directories are intentionally committed and auto-regenerated by `bun run build` from `source/skills/impeccable/`, so the effective trust surface is the 36 source skills, not the 468 fanout copies. The 1 prompt-injection scan signal in CLAUDE.md (L288) is meta-documentation about the harness inlining `SKILL.md` into a system prompt — it is NOT an injection payload, it is a developer note. Tessl Skill Review CI runs an external skill-quality reviewer on PRs touching skill content, providing a layer of automated review over manual-PR-only governance. Closed issue #100 (4d ago) explicitly added prompt-injection warnings to the `critique` skill — the maintainer is aware of the threat model and is hardening it iteratively.
entries.what_this_means: Trusting `impeccable` skills means trusting (a) pbakaus's curation of the 36 skill files in `source/`, plus (b) the build pipeline that fans them out to 13 harnesses. The fan-out factor (×13) does not multiply the trust surface — it amplifies the consequence of single-key compromise (F0). For consumers who only use one harness, the relevant directory is a single 36-file collection.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of a current injection or compromise — the existing skill content is normal design-coaching instructions written by pbakaus and AI co-authors (Claude Opus 4.7 visible as Co-Authored-By in recent commits). Not a higher trust risk than a typical 36-skill collection.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: continue Tessl Skill Review CI; consider sandboxing harness CI to enforce that `<harness>/skills/` content is byte-identical to `source/skills/impeccable/` after build (closes the regeneration drift gap). Consumer-side: read the 36 source files in `source/skills/impeccable/reference/` before installing; ignore the per-harness fanout copies.

entries.title: Scanner coverage gap on multi-harness skill collections — only AGENTS.md + CLAUDE.md (2 root files) were enumerated; 468 skill files under `<harness>/skills/` were not
entries.action_hint: Read this scan in conjunction with a manual audit of `source/skills/impeccable/` if you're sensitive to skill-content prompt-injection vectors. Same gap was documented for mattpocock/skills (catalog entry 27).
entries.threat_model_context: `docs/phase_1_harness.py::step_2_5_agent_rule_files()` enumerates known root-level rule files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, etc.) but does not recursively traverse harness subdirectories like `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` or `source/skills/<name>/reference/<command>.md`. Scanner emitted `code_patterns.agent_rule_files` length 2 (AGENTS.md L69 + CLAUDE.md L288), missing the 468-file skill content under all 13 harnesses. Same gap previously documented for catalog entry 27 (mattpocock/skills): 22 SKILL.md files not enumerated. Tracked as a V1.2.x patch candidate.
entries.what_this_means: The prompt-injection surface analysis in F4 is informed by the build-pipeline structure (single source-of-truth, regenerated mechanically), not by a per-file scan of the 468 skill files. A targeted prompt-injection scanner that recursed into `<harness>/skills/<command>/*.md` would surface more granular information.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of a vulnerability in impeccable. The gap is in the scanner harness, not in the repo under scan.
entries.how_to_fix: Scanner-side (V1.2.x patch candidate): extend `step_2_5_agent_rule_files()` to recursively enumerate `<harness>/skills/**/*.md` for known harness-name patterns (.claude, .agents, .cursor, etc.) and run prompt-injection regex over each, with a deduplication pass to detect when 13 fanout copies share the same source. Same fix would close the gap for mattpocock/skills (entry 27).

section_02.summary: Six findings: 4 Warning + 2 Info. F0–F3 cluster around the same root cause: a fast-growing solo-maintained skills surface with no structural review gate, multi-hop install paths, and no public disclosure channel. F4 is informational — the prompt-injection trust surface is large in count (468 files) but narrow in source (one `source/skills/impeccable/` directory). F5 is a scanner-side gap.

section_03.pill_row: 24,298 stars • 1,259 forks • 5.5 months old • Apache-2.0 • 96.9% solo

groups.headline: Caution — F0..F3 cluster around solo-maintainer + multi-hop install + silent disclosure on a fast-growing skills+CLI surface
groups.one_line_summary: 24k stars + 5.5 months old + zero branch protection + 3-hop install path + no GHSA channel + open build-time RCE with authored fix sitting = trust-the-maintainer-and-the-CLI posture; safe to use the CLI directly, take the slower install path for the skills.

items.claim: pbakaus 96.9% commit share + zero branch protection + 39% self-merge rate. Single-key compromise reaches 13 harnesses across the install base.
Open #92: 3 sites of `new Function()` build-time RCE in scripts/. Affects contributors (`bun run build`/`dev`), not consumers. Fix-PR authored, sitting 22 days.
`npx impeccable skills install` → `npx skills add` → vercel-labs/skills → GitHub. `skills update` fetches `impeccable.style/api/download/bundle/universal` over HTTPS with no signature verification.
0 GHSA published, no SECURITY.md, no dependabot.yml. 4 security issues handled internally (2 closed in ~7d, 2 open). Consumers don't get Dependabot alerts.
468 skill files × 13 harnesses regenerated from one source `source/skills/impeccable/` (36 files). Trust surface = 36 source files; ×13 fanout amplifies single-key compromise blast radius.

per_finding.narrative: Compromise of pbakaus's GitHub credentials (stolen token, hijacked session, OAuth-app abuse) lets an attacker push to `main` with no second-human gate. Tessl Skill Review and Copilot code review run on the PR but neither blocks merge. The malicious commit propagates to 13 harnesses on the next `bun run build` and gets shipped to consumers via three install paths (npx skills, CDN bundle, plugin marketplace). 24.3k stars indicates a non-trivial install base.

Attacker crafts a PR that mutates the regex-matched `ANTIPATTERNS` source region in `src/detect-antipatterns.mjs` to inject arbitrary code. After merge (which can be self-merge per F0), every contributor running `bun run build` or `bun run dev` executes the attacker's code with their filesystem access. Bound to the build environment, not the published artifact.

Path A — vercel-labs/skills compromise: attacker controls the npx-skills install pipeline; injects malicious skill content for every consumer who runs `npx impeccable skills install`. Path B — Cloudflare account / DNS hijack on impeccable.style: attacker substitutes the universal bundle download; consumers running `npx impeccable skills update` receive the substituted bundle (no signature check). Path C — compromise of pbakaus's GitHub (covered by F0).

Not an attack path — a notification-channel gap. Downstream consequence: when a real vulnerability is fixed (e.g. #100 shell-injection in late April), no GHSA is published, so Dependabot doesn't notify users running an older `impeccable` CLI version. Users continue running the vulnerable version indefinitely unless they manually check the repo's commits.

Threat is amplification of F0, not a separate vector. Compromise of `source/skills/impeccable/` (via F0 single-key compromise) propagates to all 13 harnesses on the next build. Tessl Skill Review exists as automated mitigation but is not a blocking gate.
Scanner-side coverage gap; no threat path against the repo under scan. Documented for V1.2.x patch.

executable_file_inventory.lead: Two CI workflow files were classified as 'executable' by the Phase 1 harness (.github/workflows/ci.yml, .github/workflows/skill-review.yml). The harness's executable-file detector did not classify the package's JS executables (no shebangs on the imported `.mjs` modules), so the security-relevant binaries below come from manual inspection of `bin/`, `src/`, and `scripts/`. The published npm package ships only `bin/`, `src/`, and `LICENSE` per `package.json` `files` declaration.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: 5 executable files of consequence: `bin/cli.js` (Node CLI entrypoint, ~57 lines), `bin/commands/skills.mjs` (skills install/update — uses `execSync` for `npx skills add` + `unzip` + `git status`; ~649 lines), `src/detect-antipatterns.mjs` (detector engine — 3,600 lines, loaded by build scripts via `new Function()` per F1), `src/detect-antipatterns-browser.js` (generated browser variant), and `scripts/build.js` (Bun build orchestrator — uses `new Function()` per F1; not in published npm package). Plus 2 GitHub Actions workflows (`ci.yml`, `skill-review.yml`).

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: The published npm package (`impeccable@2.1.8`) ships only `bin/`, `src/`, and `LICENSE` per the `files` whitelist in `package.json`. The `scripts/` directory containing the F1 `new Function()` build-time RCE is NOT shipped to consumers — it lives only in the GitHub repo and runs only when contributors execute `bun run build` or `bun run dev`.

pr_sample_review.intro: Sample = population: 49 closed PRs in lifetime (46 merged, 3 closed without merge). Formal-review rate (GitHub reviewDecision set) = 36.7% (18/49). Any-review rate (reviewDecision set OR a review event posted) = 53.1% (26/49). Self-merge count = 19 of 49 (38.8%). Security-flagged (title contains a security keyword) = 1 (open). Tessl Skill Review (CI on PRs touching skills) and GitHub Copilot code review (CI on PRs generally) both run on every PR but neither is a required-review gate. A representative sample of recent PRs:

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: Partly — 53% any-review on n=49, but solo maintainer with no branch protection and 39% self-merge rate; Tessl + Copilot CI provides automated review only
does_anyone_check_the_code.rationale: Phase 3 advisory says AMBER (RULE-2) because 36.7% formal review and 53.1% any-review on n=49 closed PRs is in the AMBER band. Calibrated answer agrees with AMBER. The amber color reflects the genuine split: automated review (Tessl + Copilot CI on every PR per E8) plus 53% community-or-self review, against a no-structural-protection backdrop (no branch protection, solo CODEOWNERS, 39% self-merge rate per E2). RULE-2 base table fires correctly; no override needed. See F0 for the structural narrative.

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: Partly — 22-day-old open #92 build-time RCE has an authored fix-PR sitting; 2 prior security issues closed within ~7-10 days in late April
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.rationale: Phase 3 advisory says RED (FALLBACK) because `oldest_open_security_item_age_days = 22` triggers the many-open-or-old branch. The RED color over-claims by ignoring the close-rate counter-signal: 2 security issues (#100 shell-injection + #93 selector-escaping per E4) were CLOSED within ~7-10 days in late April. The 22-day-old open #92 has an authored fix-PR (per E3) — it is awaiting merge, not awaiting investigation. Calibrated answer is AMBER ('they're responsive and currently mid-fix on the open item'), not RED ('they ignore security issues').
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.edge_case: Same closed-within-window counter-signal pattern previously fired on multica (catalog entry 28). The Phase 3 compute does not yet capture 'responsive_close_rate' as an explicit counter-signal — V13-3 deferred Item C9 (split `missing_qualitative_context` into `sample_floor_degeneracy` + `responsive_close_rate_counter_signal`) would land this rule at the algorithm level. Tracked in v12-wild-scan-telemetry §9. This is the 3rd `missing_qualitative_context` Q2 fire; same driver as multica (not a new-driver escalation).
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.suggested_threshold_adjustment: Add a `q2_closed_security_issues_30d` signal that counts security-tagged issues closed in the trailing 30 days. When `>=2` fires AND `oldest_open_security_item_age_days < 30`, soften the FALLBACK from RED to AMBER. Lands V13-3 deferred Item C9 (responsive_close_rate_counter_signal). Same adjustment proposed by multica (entry 28); now 3 cumulative fires (skills #27 sample-floor + multica #28 close-rate + impeccable #29 close-rate). The split itself is overdue.

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: No — 0 published GHSA, no SECURITY.md, no dependabot.yml; security fixes ship silently from the consumer's POV (no Dependabot alert, no npm advisory)
do_they_tell_you_about_problems.rationale: Phase 3 advisory says RED (FALLBACK no-disclosure). Calibrated answer agrees with RED. Per E5: zero published advisories, no SECURITY.md, no dependabot.yml. Per E4: 4 security issues handled internally with `security:` prefix in titles but never published to the GHSA channel. From a downstream-consumer POV, the disclosure channel is fully absent — Dependabot will not alert when a fix lands; you'd need to read the commit log manually. F3 is the calibrated-narrative finding.

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: Partly — install-path multi-hop (npx impeccable skills install → vercel-labs/skills npm CLI → GitHub) with no signature verification on the bundle path; CLI-only path (`npx impeccable detect`) is single-hop and safe to run
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.rationale: Phase 3 advisory says AMBER (FALLBACK install_warns). Calibrated answer agrees with AMBER. The npm package is unpinned (no integrity hash in the documented install command), `artifact_verified=false`, and `coverage_affirmations.distribution_channels_verified='1/24'` — but 23 of those 24 channels are test fixtures (per E9), not real distribution channels. The single real channel (`npm install impeccable@2.1.8`) does have an HTTPS-pinned npm registry but no PR/CI verification of the npm artifact upstream. F2 is the calibrated-narrative finding for the multi-hop install topology. Override recorded against the channels-count signal interpretation.
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.edge_case: Distribution-channel detection emitted 24 channels but 23 are TEST FIXTURES (vite8-react-* variants used as antipattern-detection test inputs, not real distributable npm packages). The real channel count is 1 (`impeccable@2.1.8`). The `q4_all_channels_pinned` and `q4_artifact_verified` signals are factually correct (no pinning, no artifact verification) but the count denominator is misleading.
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.suggested_threshold_adjustment: Add a per-channel `is_test_fixture` flag derived from path-pattern matching (e.g. `tests/`, `fixtures/`, `demos/landing/`) so distribution_channels.channels can filter to actual published packages before computing Q4 signals. Avoids misleading 'X/Y channels verified' coverage stats on monorepos with framework-fixture harnesses.

catalog_metadata.shape: Multi-harness agent-skills collection (13 harness directories × 36 skill files = 468 skill files in disjoint directories) + npm CLI for design anti-pattern detection + Chrome devtools extension. Single source-of-truth at `source/skills/impeccable/` regenerated per harness via `bun run build`.
catalog_metadata.short_description: Curated collection of 36 design-and-anti-pattern skills (slash commands) shipped to 13 different AI coding harnesses (.claude, .cursor, .gemini, .agents, .github, .kiro, .opencode, .pi, .qoder, .rovodev, .trae, .trae-cn, plus a `plugin/` Claude Code plugin marketplace bundle) plus a Node CLI (`impeccable detect`) for headless anti-pattern scanning, plus a Chrome devtools extension. By Paul Bakaus.
catalog_metadata.target_user: Frontend designer or developer using an AI coding agent who wants to catch AI-generated design slop (italic-serif hero typography, hero metric stacks, gradient text, glassmorphism, generic card grids) and apply consistent design heuristics. Three install paths: (1) `npx impeccable skills install` — delegates to vercel-labs/skills npm CLI which fetches from GitHub; (2) Claude Code plugin marketplace via `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`; (3) `npx impeccable detect <files-or-dir>` for one-shot CLI scans without persistent skill installation.
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: First scan of pbakaus/impeccable. 14th wild V1.2-schema scan after markitdown 15, ghostty 16, Kronos 17, kamal 18, Xray-core 19, browser_terminal 20, wezterm 21, QuickLook 22, kanata 23, freerouting 24, WLED 25, Baileys 26, skills 27, multica 28.

editorial_caption.text: pbakaus/impeccable — 24.3k-star design-anti-pattern detection toolkit shipped as 36 skill files fanned out to 13 different AI coding harnesses (.claude, .cursor, .gemini, .agents, etc.) plus a Node CLI plus a Chrome extension. Created 5.5 months ago, single-author (96.9% share), no branch protection, 38.8% self-merge rate. 4 security issues have been triaged (2 closed in ~7-10 days, 2 open with fix-PRs sitting). Open #92 is a build-time RCE in 3 build scripts via `new Function()`; affects contributors only, not consumers of the published CLI. Install topology is 3-hop for skills (`npx impeccable skills install` → vercel-labs/skills → GitHub) with no signature verification on the alternative CDN bundle path; CLI-only `npx impeccable detect [paths]` is single-hop and safe. 0 published GHSA + no SECURITY.md = consumers won't get Dependabot alerts when fixes ship. The skill-content surface (468 files) amplifies single-key compromise blast radius but trusts only one source-of-truth directory (`source/skills/impeccable/`, 36 files).

body_paragraphs: pbakaus is the only contributor with maintainer access to a 24.3k-star, 5.5-month-old project. The remaining ~3% of contributions come from external community PR authors but no other account has merge rights. CODEOWNERS at `.github/CODEOWNERS` reads `* @pbakaus` for global ownership plus specific rules for `scripts/` and `source/` (also `@pbakaus`). 19 of 49 closed PRs (38.8%) were self-merged.

All 7 governance signals are negative. Classic branch protection returns HTTP 404 (no rule). 0 rulesets configured; 0 rules-on-default-branch. The owner is a User account, not an Org, so `org_rulesets` is N/A. The repo is not indexed by OSSF Scorecard. The Tessl Skill Review and Copilot code review CI workflows do run on every PR (visible in `.github/workflows/`) but neither is a required-review gate — pbakaus can self-merge any PR and frequently does.

If pbakaus's GitHub credentials are compromised — stolen token, hijacked session, OAuth-app abuse — an attacker can push directly to `main` with no second-human gate. The malicious commit propagates to 13 harness directories on the next `bun run build` and reaches consumers via three install paths: `npx impeccable skills install`, `npx impeccable skills update` (CDN bundle), and the Claude Code plugin marketplace. 24.3k stars indicates a non-trivial install base. Mitigation is consumer-side: pin to a specific commit SHA when installing, audit `source/skills/impeccable/` before linking, or use only the CLI (`npx impeccable detect`) which doesn't pull skill content.

Issue #92 'security: replace new Function() eval with direct ESM import' was filed 22 days ago and is still open. The PR is authored — the fix replaces 3 instances of `new Function()` code evaluation with direct ESM imports of the already-exported `ANTIPATTERNS` array — but has not been merged. The companion meta-tracking issue #94 'Security and consistency audit findings' (also 22 days open) classifies this as 'High' severity.

The three sites are `scripts/build.js:122` (validateAntipatternRules — runs during `bun run build`), `scripts/build-extension.js:63` (antipatterns.json extraction — runs during extension build), and `scripts/lib/sub-pages-data.js:107` (readAntipatternRules — runs at dev-server boot AND during build). All three extract a regex-matched region of `src/detect-antipatterns.mjs` and evaluate it with `new Function()`. Any commit that mutates the matched region grants arbitrary code execution in the build environment when `bun run build` or `bun run dev` runs.

The impact is bounded to repository contributors and CI. The published `impeccable` npm package ships only `bin/`, `src/`, and `LICENSE` (per `package.json` `files`); the `scripts/` directory is not in the package. Consumers running `npx impeccable detect` or `npx impeccable live` do not invoke the build scripts. So this is a developer-local RCE, not a published-package supply-chain RCE. The fix landing would close it; until then, contributors should be aware that `bun run build` + `bun run dev` execute trusted code only — review PRs touching `src/detect-antipatterns.mjs` carefully before pulling them.

The skills install path is documented as `npx impeccable skills install`. Reading `bin/commands/skills.mjs` line 339 reveals this is implemented as `execSync('npx skills add pbakaus/impeccable')` — i.e. the `impeccable` CLI delegates to a third-party npm CLI (`vercel-labs/skills`) which then fetches from GitHub. Compromise of `vercel-labs/skills` allows injection of malicious skill content for every consumer who triggers an install.

The alternative path is `npx impeccable skills update`, which calls `downloadAndExtractBundle()` to fetch `https://impeccable.style/api/download/bundle/universal` over HTTPS, then unpacks with `execSync('unzip -qo "${tmpZip}" -d "${tmpDir}"')`. There is no signature verification: no SHA hash, no GPG, no Sigstore — just TLS to the impeccable.style Cloudflare Pages origin. Compromise of the Cloudflare account or DNS hijack of impeccable.style allows skill substitution for every consumer who runs `skills update`.

Install path 3 (Claude Code plugin marketplace via `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`) follows Anthropic's plugin-marketplace trust model — single-source from this GitHub repo. The CLI-only path `npx impeccable detect <files>` does NOT pull skill content and is single-hop (npm registry → consumer); it is the safest install option if you only need the anti-pattern detector and don't want skills.

0 GitHub Security Advisories have been published despite 4 visible security-tagged issues being actively triaged. Two security issues were closed within ~7-10 days in late April 2026: #100 addressed shell injection in `npx impeccable [target]`, port-squatting in `npx impeccable live`, and added prompt-injection warnings to the critique skill; #93 hardened `extension/devtools/panel.js:503-511` against selector-injection by switching from manual escaping to `JSON.stringify`. Both fixes landed via maintainer self-merge.

Two security issues remain open as of scan date (both 22 days old): #92 (build-time RCE — see F1) has an authored fix-PR sitting, and #94 (audit tracking issue) lists multiple medium-severity items. No SECURITY.md exists at the repo root, so there's no public channel for coordinated disclosure (the maintainer uses GitHub Issues with explicit `security:` title prefix as an ad-hoc channel). No `.github/dependabot.yml` exists either — dependency security patches arrive via manual PR rather than automated bot.

From a downstream consumer's perspective: when a security fix ships in a new `impeccable@x.y.z` release, npm advisory database doesn't know about it (no GHSA), Dependabot doesn't notify (no advisory feed), and the npm-package release-notes channel isn't picked up by typical security-monitoring tools. You learn about security fixes by reading the repo's commit log or the impeccable.style changelog manually. The fixes themselves ARE transparent (issues + PRs are public) — the gap is in the notification channel, not the disclosure of facts.

Each `.md` file under any of the 13 `<harness>/skills/<command>/` subtrees becomes part of an LLM's instruction context when its harness loads the skill. 36 unique skills × 13 harness fanouts = 468 skill files in the working tree. CLAUDE.md L96 documents that the harness output directories are intentionally committed and auto-regenerated by `bun run build` from `source/skills/impeccable/`.

The effective trust surface is the 36 source files in `source/skills/impeccable/reference/`, not the 468 fanout copies. The build pipeline does provider-specific placeholder replacement (`{{model}}`, `{{config_file}}`, `{{ask_instruction}}`, `{{command_prefix}}`, `{{available_commands}}`, `{{scripts_path}}` per CLAUDE.md L86-93) but otherwise the skill text is identical across harnesses. Reading the 36 source files is sufficient to audit the post-install instruction surface.

The 1 prompt-injection scan signal in CLAUDE.md L288 ('The harness inlines `SKILL.md` into the system prompt for "skill-on"...') is meta-documentation about the consumer-side instruction-loading mechanism — not an injection payload. Tessl Skill Review CI runs an external skill-quality reviewer on PRs touching skill content, providing an automated review layer (but not a blocking gate; see F0). Closed issue #100 (4 days ago) explicitly added prompt-injection warnings to the `critique` skill — the maintainer is aware of the threat model and is hardening it iteratively.

The Phase 1 harness (`docs/phase_1_harness.py::step_2_5_agent_rule_files()`) enumerates known root-level rule files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, etc.) but does not recursively traverse harness subdirectories. For impeccable, this means only the 2 root files (AGENTS.md L69 + CLAUDE.md L288) were captured; the 468 skill `.md` files under all 13 `<harness>/skills/<command>/` subtrees were not enumerated.

Same gap previously documented for catalog entry 27 (mattpocock/skills): 22 SKILL.md files not enumerated. The fix is a V1.2.x patch candidate: extend `step_2_5_agent_rule_files()` to recursively enumerate `<harness>/skills/**/*.md` for known harness-name patterns (.claude, .agents, .cursor, .gemini, .kiro, .opencode, .pi, .qoder, .rovodev, .trae, .trae-cn, .github, plugin) and run prompt-injection regex over each, with a deduplication pass that detects when N fanout copies share the same source content.

The F4 finding's prompt-injection surface analysis is informed by the build-pipeline structure (single source-of-truth, regenerated mechanically) per CLAUDE.md L94-102, not by a per-file scan of the 468 skill files. A targeted scanner would surface more granular information.
